summary: Martin Fowler explains the on-site customer practice from Extreme Programming, which requires a business representative to be physically present with the development team. The customer answers questions, interacts with developers, and is considered a full team member whose contribution is as vital as the developers'. This person comes from the business side, not development, and is responsible for making decisions about business value and describing requirements. While they don't abandon their regular job, physical presence is mandatory, and the role is often filled by a team rather than an individual.
key_points: The on-site customer must be physically present with developers, not remote or occasionally available
Customers are full team members whose success contribution equals that of developers
The customer role belongs to business beneficiaries, not development staff
Customers make business value decisions and describe requirements to the team
The customer role is commonly filled by a team rather than a single person
money_quotes: Indeed they are part of the development team, and recognize that the success of the team depends as much on them as it does on the developers.
The XP customer is from the business side of the organization not the development side.
They don't have to give up their regular job to do this, but they must be physically present.
The customer is someone who will be a beneficiary of the system.
key_insight: True customer involvement in XP means physical presence and equal responsibility for team success, not occasional consultation from the business side.
